ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw goose liver as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for poultry organ meats is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw goose liver is approximately 85 g, equivalent to a small individual portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Goose, liver, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB No. 05144
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11050001). Saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for goose liver, raw (NDB 05144) to complete secondary nutrient fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — organ meat with no GI assignment in peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Goose liver, raw contains 6.32 g carbohydrate per 100 g, primarily as glycogen. Organ meats are not assigned a GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed GI literature because the carbohydrate content is insufficient to produce a meaningful postprandial glucose response at normal serving sizes. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a sentinel numeric value indicating not-applicable status; gi_category is set to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 as GI cannot be meaningfully applied to this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (140 mg/100 g) and very high cholesterol (515 mg/100 g) with cardiovascular implications
hypertension.guidance: Raw goose liver has moderate sodium at 140 mg/100 g and a favourable potassium ratio (230 mg/100 g), but its very high cholesterol content (515 mg/100 g) raises cardiovascular risk in hypertensive patients; limit portion size and avoid high-sodium preparations such as pâté or cured foie gras.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (16.4 g/100 g), low sugar (0 g), moderate carbohydrate (6.3 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact at normal serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Goose liver, raw is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its high protein content, negligible sugar, and low glycaemic impact; however, very high cholesterol (515 mg/100 g) and very high iron (30.5 mg/100 g) mean that large or frequent servings should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high dietary cholesterol (515 mg/100 g) and organ meat arachidonic acid precursors with pro-inflammatory lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Goose liver, raw is high concern for cardiovascular disease due to its exceptionally high cholesterol content (515 mg/100 g) and organ-meat lipid profile; individuals with established CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption to occasional small portions and avoid high-fat preparations such as foie gras or cream-based pâté.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high cholesterol and organ-meat arachidonic acid precursors that amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Goose liver, raw is not recommended for regular consumption in rheumatoid arthritis due to its pro-inflammatory lipid profile (515 mg/100 g cholesterol, arachidonic acid precursors) and absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory factors; occasional small portions may be tolerated but regular intake should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Goose liver, raw is an uncooked organ meat sourced from the domestic goose (Anser anser domesticus), characterised by a reddish-brown colour, smooth surface, and dense, soft texture. It is nutritionally dense, providing 133 kcal per 100 g with 16.4 g protein, 4.3 g fat, and 6.3 g carbohydrate (primarily as glycogen). It is an exceptional source of iron (30.5 mg/100 g — more than 100% of the adult RDI), vitamin B12 (54 mcg/100 g), folate (738 mcg DFE/100 g), vitamin A (977 mcg RAE/100 g), and selenium (67.1 mcg/100 g). However, it contains very high dietary cholesterol at 515 mg/100 g, making it a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. Clinically, it is suitable in small portions for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high protein content, negligible sugar, and low glycaemic impact, but its cholesterol load requires portion control. In culinary contexts, raw goose liver is used as the base ingredient for foie gras (force-fed goose liver), pâté de foie gras, and various European liver preparations. It is most commonly associated with French and Central European cuisine. The food is classified as raw and minimally processed, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is not suitable for vegetarian or vegan diets.
common_user_queries: How much iron is in goose liver?
Is goose liver good for diabetes?
Is raw goose liver safe to eat?
Goose liver vs chicken liver nutrition
Can I eat goose liver if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11050001, confidence 0.95) with secondary micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for goose liver, raw (NDB 05144). Both are approved primary sources. The food identity is unambiguous and the nutrient profile is internally consistent.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; goose liver is an organ meat with no plant-based fibre content — naturally zero. sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; liver glycogen does not register as free sugar — confirmed zero. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as goose liver contains negligible industrially produced trans fats per USDA SR Legacy — confirmed zero. vitamin_d_mcg and vitamin_e_mg set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry and are not available in USDA SR Legacy NDB 05144 with sufficient precision for this record. Saturated fat (1.43 g), monounsaturated fat (1.04 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.65 g), vitamin C (6.0 mg), vitamin A (977 mcg RAE), thiamine (0.54 mg), riboflavin (1.14 mg), niacin (6.0 mg), vitamin B6 (0.74 mg), vitamin B12 (54.0 mcg), folate (738 mcg DFE), phosphorus (261 mg), magnesium (25 mg), zinc (3.72 mg), selenium (67.1 mcg), and water (72.0 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05144 (goose, liver, raw) to supplement HPB primary values. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel value indicating not-applicable status per validator requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable and gi_source documents that organ meats are not assigned GI values in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed GI literature. Halal status is marked false as goose liver requires specific halal slaughter certification that cannot be assumed for a generic raw ingredient record. Ontology: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03311737) and FoodEx2 code (A0FJP) are defensible best-match assignments for goose liver, raw; SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented. This is a generic global ingredient, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory local-language naming is required under G6.

All three mandatory health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with specific clinical guidance. Multilingual naming is not required as this is a generic global ingredient. All zero and -1 sentinel values are documented in notes. Aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object.
